texts.text: 1.1. Global lithium extraction status and key issues of Chinese salt-lake brines

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Lithium  and  its  compounds  have  many  industrial  applications including nuclear fusion, pharmaceuticals, chemical and metallurgical industries, and particularly in rechargeable lithium-ion batteries (LIBs) for consumable electronics, electric vehicles (EVs) and energy storage [1 -3]. For the total 14 million tons (t) of exploitable lithium reserve worldwide  (as  Li  metal)  (Fig.  1A)  [4],  most  are  present  in  salt-lake brines, distributed in the United States of America, China, and South America ' s  lithium  triangle of  Chile, Argentina and Bolivia [5]. Some lithium  ores  are  located  in  West  Australia  and  Southwest  China [4].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Interests on lithium extraction from the Chinese salt-lake brines have regained momentum due to the growing demand of lithium for application in electric vehicles. Characterized by a high Mg 2 + /Li + ratio (MLR), the Chinese salt-lake brine has troubled scientists and engineers for decades. Recent progress has shown significant advantages  of  membrane  technologies.  This  review  summarizes  the  membrane  technologies  utilized  for  lithium extraction,  including  membrane  extraction  (ME),  nanofiltration  (NF),  lithium  ion-sieve  (LIS),  electrodialysis (ED), and forward osmosis (FO). The fundamental concepts and separation mechanisms of ME and chemical exchange are introduced. Positive NF membranes, ion-selective ED membranes are discussed in detail in terms of materials design for high permselectivity. Maintaining the high selectivity and adsorption capacity are discussed for LIS membranes. Solutions to design single ion-selective separation membranes by hybrid of mobility and affinity  are discussed for the urgent lithium extraction problem. Carbon neutral FO is proposed for the concentration of lithium-enriched brines with a focal on a low structure parameter substrate and a high rejection for highly saline brine. The fundamental and engineering strategies for lithium extraction from high MLR brine will be of strong interest for the harvesting of valuable metal resources from complicated brine.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: For the healthy growth of EVs, extraction of lithium from brine has become the key important supply. Salt-lake brines are characterized by their geological structure. For extraction of lithium, one of the key parameters  determining  the  lithium  extraction  process  is  the  ratio  of magnesium and lithium in brines, known as Mg 2 + /Li + ratio (MLR). At low  MLR,  a ' Lime-Soda-Evaporation ' process  is  the  most  selected technology for MLR of 0.1 -10. Approximately 80% of lithium has been recovered from brines based on this technology, i.e. South America ' s lithium  triangle  of  Chile,  Argentina  and  Bolivia  [5,11].  The  general process  is  as  follows:  brines  are  pumped  into  shallow  ponds  for one-year-long  solar  evaporation  to  precipitate  out  the  chlorides  and sulfates  of  K + ,  Na + ,  Mg 2 + ,  Ca 2 + et  al.,  then  excess  lime  is  added  to remove Mg 2 + and finally with Na2CO3 to produce the insoluble Li2CO3 (purity above 99%) [12,13]. The process has also been used to extract lithium  from  Zabuye  Salt  Lake  in  China  (MLR < 0.1)  [12].  Besides, lithium extraction from Hombre Muerto Salt Lake in Argentina (MLR ~ 1.5)  employed  selective  adsorption  and  solar  evaporation  combined technology, and FMC company adopted this technology and reached the LCE capacity of 20,000 tons/year as early as 1996 [12].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Over 80% of lithium reserve in China is present in salt-lake brine (Fig. 1B). A recent survey shows that the LCE reserve in the Qinghai saltlake  brine  alone  is  more  than  10  million  tons  [5].  Considering  the recycling of the used LIBs, a closed-loop sustainable supply of lithium chemical is envisioned, providing that efficient Li + separation technology is available. Although the widely adopted ' Lime-Soda-Evaporation ' process has shown success in lithium recovery from brine, extracting lithium from the Chinese salt-lake brine is most difficult due to the very large MLR in brine (Table 1). On average (Table 1), the MLR is ~20, but in Qarhan salt-lake, the MLR reaches 365, at least a magnitude higher than the average. Besides, other countries also have salt-lake brines with high MLR, such as Chott Djerid Lake (Tunisia), where MLR reaches 57 [15].  The  conventional ' Lime-Soda-Precipitation ' lithium  extraction process is  unviable  considering the  cost, low  efficiency and  negative environmental  impact  of  discharged  solid  wastes  [13].  Decades  of research and development efforts have been paid in China to find scalable solutions of low cost and low energy demand for the sustainable supply of LCE.

texts.orig: The difficulties in separating Mg 2 + from Li + lie in their similar ionic radii,  either  in  bare  or  hydration  states  (Table  2)  [22 -26]. The bare radius of Li + (0.068 nm) is only slightly larger than that of Mg 2 + (0.065 nm), but comparably smaller than that of Na + (0.095 nm), K + (0.133 nm) and Ca 2 + (0.099 nm). The hydration number of Li + is 5, but the lifetime or exchange rate of water molecules in the hydration layer is 5 × 10 9 s, whilst Mg 2 + has a hydration number of 6 with a significantly much slower exchange rate of 10 6 s. The resulting hydration radius of Li + is 0.38 nm, slightly smaller than that of Mg 2 + (0.43 nm). Although Na + , K + and Ca 2 + ions all show similar hydration ion diameter, to Li + ions, pre-treatment during the evaporating process to produce KCl fertilizer (Fig. 2A -D) has reduced the concentration of these ions, thus not a critical issue for lithium carbonate production.

At this stage, the lithium concentration is about 0.02 -0.03 g L 1 ,  still  too  low  to  be  of  significant commercial value. Sequential evaporation ponds will then enrich the brine by solar or wind power and NaCl precipitates first followed by KCl (Fig. 2B -D). texts.orig: Fig. 2E -H summarizes the most recent technologies utilized or under intensive  research  for  extracting  lithium  from  the  Chinese  salt-lake brine. In general, the extracting process consists of two steps: Mg 2 + / Li + separation to purify Li + to obtain lithium-enriched brine, and concentration of the Li + enriched brine to produce either LiCl or Li2CO3. The  first  technically  successful  technology  has  been  liquid-liquid extraction  (LLE)  (Fig.  2E1)  which  will  be  detailed  in  the  following paragraph.  Concerns  on  the  negative  environmental  impact  led  to modern  membrane  technologies  ranging  from  electrodialysis  (ED) (Fig. 2F2) and nanofiltration (NF) (Fig. 2F3) for Mg 2 + /Li + separation, to reverse  osmosis  (RO)  for  concentration  (Fig.  2G2).  New  membrane development foresaw membrane extraction (ME) for overcoming the negative  environmental issue of LLE (Fig.  2E2) and forward osmosis (FO) for process intensification, energy-saving as well as cost reduction (Fig. 2G1). The related performances of the above membrane technologies  are  shown  in  Table  3.  The  key  criteria  for  various  membrane technologies will be introduced and the future development outlined in the following.

texts.text: The mechanism for Li + separation and concentration using LLE or ME is based on equilibrium. The other technologies (Fig. 2F2-F3) are unanimously separation process based on non-equilibrium. To close the loop  for  Li + extraction,  two  stages  of  very  different  purposes  are employed:

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: (1)  A separation/purification stage to reduce the MLR down to one digit so that chemical precipitation is viable. This can be achieved through  lithium  ion-sieves  (LISs)  (Fig.  2F1).  However,  LIS breakage leads to decreased adsorption efficiency and the loss of absorbent.  Thus,  the  LIS-loaded  membrane  absorber  has  been developed with the potential for selective absorption of Li + [29, 30]. ED and NF have been extensively studied and implemented for  separating  Li + from  Mg 2 + (Fig.  2F2  and  Fig.  2F3)  [27]. Commercial NF and ion exchange membranes (IEMs) can separate Mg 2 + and Li + to industrial-grade due to the relatively low permselectivity. texts.orig: This perspective review aims to introduce the state-of-the-art technologies, which are used in lithium extraction from the salt-lake brines in China, focusing on membrane technologies because of their intrinsic low energy demand and high separation efficiency. Both separation and concentration stages are discussed with the aim to produce either LiCl or Li2CO3. The review further provides a critical analysis of the mainstream separation  mechanisms  and  the  contemporary  development  in  membrane materials and processes. The membrane  technologies are reviewed for the equilibrium separation using ME technology and nonequilibrium separation using NF, ED membranes. The design principles for  membranes  with  different  purposes  are  critically  analyzed.  For concentration purposes, this review will introduce FO as the key membrane technology for the concentration of Li + enriched brine. Guidelines for improving the performance of all membrane materials are discussed. The perspective on novel membrane materials and membrane processes showcases the key importance of membrane technologies in separating the most difficult ions and innovating the process intensification.

The review further provides a critical analysis of the mainstream separation  mechanisms  and  the  contemporary  development  in  membrane materials and processes. The membrane  technologies are reviewed for the equilibrium separation using ME technology and nonequilibrium separation using NF, ED membranes. The design principles for  membranes  with  different  purposes  are  critically  analyzed.  For concentration purposes, this review will introduce FO as the key membrane technology for the concentration of Li + enriched brine. Guidelines for improving the performance of all membrane materials are discussed. The perspective on novel membrane materials and membrane processes showcases the key importance of membrane technologies in separating the most difficult ions and innovating the process intensification.

texts.orig: 2.1. Li + extraction, chemical exchange and stripping
texts.text: 2.1. Li + extraction, chemical exchange and stripping

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Fig.  3A  shows  a  continuous  lithium  extraction  process  from  an enriched brine, consisting of three equilibrium steps: extraction, chemical exchange and stripping, which can lead to a final product with a LiCl concentration as high as 20 wt% (~200 g L 1 ). The chemical reactions in each step are illustrated in Scheme 1. In the extraction step, the lithium ions  in  the  aqueous  phase  form  complexes  with  extractants  in  the organic  phase.  In  the  chemical  exchange  stage,  the  lithium-loaded organic phase is further in contact with an aqueous phase containing both Li + and Mg 2 + .  Consequently,  lithium  is  further  enriched  in  the organic phase and magnesium in the aqueous phase. In the stripping stage, the lithium-enriched organic phase is stripped using a strong acid, such as HCl. In the stripping step, a concentrated acid corresponds to very high Li + content ending with a highly concentrated LiCl acid solution of about 20 wt%, which is then further processed for the production of LiCl by evaporation of acid. As an example, from a brine containing Li + ions of 0.3 g L 1 to 20 wt% LiCl (around 30 g L 1 Li + ), the enrichment factor for Li + is 100. The unique feature of LLE is that the extraction and stripping steps work both for the enrichment of Li + ions from a low content to a very high content, which is independent of the initial Li + concentration in the brine.

texts.orig: For purification of Li + from Mg 2 + , a chemical exchange concept is adopted from isotope separation practice, i.e. 6 Li/ 7 Li separation. Both 6 Li  and 7 Li  are  important  elements  for  the  nuclear  energy  industry [50 -53]. The chemical exchange process is an equilibrium separation technology [52,54,55]. Since typical organic extractants have 7 Li/ 6 Li separation factors in the range of 1.01 -1.05 [56], a cascade process of a few hundred stages is needed to enrich 7 Li from a natural abundance of 92.5% -99.99% [56]. In contrast, there exist many organic extractants for Mg/Li separation with separation factors of two orders of magnitude as high [57], which renders high feasibility for Li + separation from Mg 2 + using chemical exchange (Fig. 3A, D).

texts.orig: Thermodynamically, to form a complex with an organic extractant, dehydration or partial dehydration of ions incurs a steep energy barrier for the extraction reaction (Fig. 3B). This determines the kinetics of the reaction, but the selectivity is determined by ( Δ G Mg Δ G Li), as shown in Eq. (1). The chemical exchange reaction of Mg 2 + and Li + takes place at the organic/aqueous phase interface (Fig. 3D). When the chemical exchange Gibbs free energy is negative (Scheme 1 (iii), Eq. (3)), Li + ions in the acid aqueous phase will replace Mg 2 + ions in the organic phase and upon equilibrium, the organic phase is loaded with more Li + ions and the purity could be controlled using the model of chemical exchange (Supplementary  Information  S1.2).  This  process  is  vital  because, without it, it is practically impossible to purify lithium from the high MLR salt-lake brine.

texts.orig: The Born ion transfer energy from the aqueous to organic phase is always positive as the aqueous phase has a significantly larger permittivity ( ϵ 1 > ϵ 2) [23]. Because Mg 2 + and Li + ions have very similar radius but different charges, the Born transfer energy for Mg 2 + is estimated to be about 4 times that of Li + . As such, Li + is preferentially transferred to the organic phase. The molecular structures of some typical neutral Li + extractants  are  shown  in  Fig.  4,  including  phosphate-based  (tributyl phosphate (TBP), trioctylphosphine oxide (TOPO)), amide based (N503, N523) and crown ether based (B15C5, DB12C4) (see Supplementary Information Table S1 for the list of extractant chemicals and Supplementary Information S5 for the details of their fundamental reaction mechanisms). The P = O [57] or amide enhanced carbonyl moieties have high  charge  density  which  favours  strong  interactions  with  Li + ions resulting  in  stable  extraction  [58,59].  The  ion-dipole  electrostatic interaction between the Li + empty orbitals and the lone pair electrons of oxygen moieties on the crown ether leads to stable complexes as well [60,61]. When the cavity size of the ether ring matches the size of the Li + (1.3 Å), strong extraction occurs [62,63].

texts.orig: The separation efficiency of the chemical exchange, Scheme 1, reaches maximal upon equilibrium. Thus, achieving equilibrium is critical to achieving high Mg 2 + /Li + separation. Research has demonstrated that TBP extractant can purify Li + from high MLR salt-lake brines at any targeting purity in a rather short separation stage (Supplementary Information S1.2 and Fig. S3) [57]. Dissolution of organic extractant in the brine during extraction should be minimized due to the strong negative environmental impact as well as the need of replenishing the highly costly chemicals. Novel membrane extraction technologies have been developed aiming at addressing both concerns. The following session will summarize the status of ME for lithium extraction from salt-lake brine.

Dissolution of organic extractant in the brine during extraction should be minimized due to the strong negative environmental impact as well as the need of replenishing the highly costly chemicals. Novel membrane extraction technologies have been developed aiming at addressing both concerns. The following session will summarize the status of ME for lithium extraction from salt-lake brine.

texts.orig: In  the  extraction  stage,  LiCl  reacts  with  FeCl3  and  forms  LiFeCl4 which  diffuses  through  the  membrane  and  form  a  complex  of  Li [FeCl4*2TBP] (Reaction 1 and 2, Scheme 2) with TBP in the organic phase, which completes the extraction process. The stripping process is achieved by membrane exchange of the lithium loaded organic phase containing  Li[FeCl4*2TBP]  with  HCl  aqueous  phase  to  transfer  the lithium ions from the organic phase into the aqueous phase (reaction 3, Scheme 2), and the process can run continuously till a balanced status is reached. The protonated complex H[FeCl4*2TBP] in the organic phase can be regenerated by saponification via reaction with NaOH to yield Na [FeCl4*2TBP], which can then be further used for lithium membrane extraction (reaction 5, Scheme 2) [57].

texts.orig: Hydrophilic polymeric membranes have been used to separate the organic extractant from the aqueous feed to ensure that the membrane is spontaneously wetted by the aqueous phase, preventing leakage of the organic extractant, but allowing free diffusion of ions. The organic phase normally  has  low  interfacial  tension  [57],  and  may  intrude  on  the membrane pores, consequently, membranes with nanometer-sized surface  pores  are  expected.  Polyethersulfone  (PES)  and  sulfonated  poly (phthalazinone ether sulfone ketone) (SPPESK) blends have been prepared  via  immersion  precipitation  [73,74].  The  concept  is  based  on taking  advantage  of  the  rigidity  of  hydrophobic  PES  polymer  to constrain the degree of swelling of the hydrophilic sulfonated SPPESK polymer [74]. Stable performance was demonstrated in a 50/50 vol% TBP/kerosene extractant and 146 g L 1 HCl (stripping solution) for Li + extraction [74] and showed promise for separation of copper and nickel [73].

Fig. 6 shows a schematic description for the TSDM model to create a bicontinuous microstructure. When a polymer dope solution is immersed into a nonsolvent mixture, the exchange of solvent and nonsolvent results in a liquid-liquid demixing (Stage 1). The microstructure at this state is a continuous  polymer-rich  phase  confining  the  polymer-lean  phases (Fig. 6, top imbedded picture). This first-stage demixing takes place in a very short time and a closed cellular structure with high resistance is formed. However, for a polymer blend, such as PES/polyvinyl pyrrolidone (PVP) [78 -83], a second-stage demixing within the polymer-rich phase,  e.g.  demixing  of  PES/PVP,  tears  off  the  wall  of  the  closed cellular  pores,  resulting  to  open  bicontinuous  structure  or  interconnected pores (Fig. 6 bottom imbedded picture). texts.orig: The TSDM model has been used to design solvent stable membranes using  a  semicrystalline  polymer  of  poly  (ethyleneco -vinyl  alcohol) (EVAL) (Fig. 7A) [18,84]. EVAL has hydrophobic solvent stable ethylene blocks providing mechanical strength and hydrophilic ethylene alcohol blocks for water affinity. The EVAL membranes prepared as such have interconnected nanosized pores (Fig. 7A), providing a model candidate illustrating  the  two-stage  demixing  mechanism.  The  demixing  of ethylene block and ethylene alcohol block at the second stage demixing contributes  to  an  interconnected  nanoporous  structure  allowing  free diffusion  of  lithium  ions.  The  EVAL  polymer  is  micro-crystalline  as confirmed  by  Differential  Scanning  Calorimetry  (DSC)  measurement. The negligible weight change in various solvents confirms the stability of EVAL membrane in both polar and non-polar solvents [18,84]. Fig. 7B shows a very stable lithium extraction performance for more than 1000 h.

texts.orig: A  more  robust  solvent/acid  resistant  membrane  that  has  been developed recently is based on polyether ether ketone (PEEK). PEEK is semicrystalline, nearly insoluble in any common polar and apolar solvents, and resistant to strong acids [85 -88], making it an ideal candidate for acid stripping of lithium [89]. However, the membrane preparation process is rather challenging using a mixture of methanesulphonic acid (MSA) and concentrated sulfuric acid (SA) as the solvent, highly corrosive and reactive. The membrane shows a sponge top layer and macrovoid substrate with the nanoscale interconnected porous top surface (Fig. 7C). This particular morphology is ascribed to an interplay of the solid-liquid and liquid-liquid demixing upon immersion precipitation. Light sulfonation of PEEK taken place during dissolution results in hydrophilic sulfonated poly(ether ketone) (SPEEK) segments [85], which facilitate the interconnected nanoporous structure formation (Fig. 7C). The PEEK membrane is very stable in strong acid and organic extractant, with no flux change for up to 500 h of operation, but Li + flux in stripping requires  further  improvement  (Fig.  7D)  [89].  To  further  disrupt  the compact layer of the PEEK membrane, a deliberate addition of SPEEK has  demonstrated  further  reduced  crystallinity  and  improved  pore connectivity and hydrophilicity (Fig. 7E) [76]. A maximal Li + extraction flux of 4.9 × 10 8  mol cm 2 s 1 has been observed at a PEEK/SPEEK ratio of 50/50 wt% (Fig. 7F). The reduced pore tortuosity is ascribed to the presence of SPEEK, which contributes to the significant improvement in the extraction flux.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Recent development of solvent, acid resistant membranes has provided core materials for green sustainable liquid-liquid extraction of Li + from  the  high  MLR  Chinese  salt-lake  brine.  Apparently,  the  solvent resistance  membranes  are  still  porous  and  dissolution  of  organic extractant, although reduced, still exists. Besides membrane materials, technical  development of the EVAL and PEEK membranes into large modules has been attempted for lithium isotope enrichment, yet severe challenges have been faced including difficulties in potting because of their solvent and acid resistance. Mass transfer and fluid dynamics in the modules, either flat cartridges or hollow fibers, are not yet systematically  investigated.  For  brine  with  a  low  Li + ion  concentration,  the membrane surface area of the ME system will increase inverse proportionally.  A  suitable  membrane  module  still  requires  justification. Nonetheless,  hollow  fiber  membrane  extraction  contactors  typically offer 500 times more area than what is obtainable in extraction columns, leading to remarkably smaller footprints [90 -94]. It is expected that the ME system would find usage in highly concentrated brine containing a high Li + content.

texts.orig: A recent perspective from Yale proposes the use of the transition state  theory  by  Eyring  to  describe  the  solute  diffusion  in  the  active separation  layer  similar  to  elementary  reaction  [98].  Transport  of  a solute across the membrane needs to overcome a series of energy barriers associated with the pore entry and consecutive barriers within the pore. Using an Arrhenius type equation, the analogue of diffusion to reaction assumes the rate-limiting step to be the partition at the pore entrance and the diffusion through the pore (Eq. (8)). This results in an energy barrier ( E a) for solute transport (Fig. 8A). Eq. (8) shows that the solute permeability is exponentially related to E a  for solute transport across the membrane.

Transport  of  a solute across the membrane needs to overcome a series of energy barriers associated with the pore entry and consecutive barriers within the pore. Using an Arrhenius type equation, the analogue of diffusion to reaction assumes the rate-limiting step to be the partition at the pore entrance and the diffusion through the pore (Eq. (8)). This results in an energy barrier ( E a) for solute transport (Fig. 8A). Eq. (8) shows that the solute permeability is exponentially related to E a  for solute transport across the membrane.

Selectivity is further affected by electrostatic interactions and the intrinsic charges at the pore entrance, which favor counter-ions diffusion but exclude co-ions. For a solute with a hydration diameter ( d h) larger than the pore size ( d p), the necessary dehydration (or partial dehydration) sets an energy barrier as well. When the ions are partially dehydrated and enter the pore, the dielectric effect governs the energy barrier for ion permeation, which contributes to the selectivity of similar  monovalent  ions by  membranes. texts.orig: Design of a highly permselective membrane requires rational incorporation of the above mechanisms. The sieving effect has been reported using micrometer-thick graphene oxide laminates which block solutes with hydrated radii above 4.5 Å [100]. Further reducing the sieving dimension has been achieved using epoxy confinement with improved stability [101]. Although the interlayer spacing mainly contributes to the separation, the charge effect is also reported for micrometer-thick MXene  membranes.  Large  cations  with  hydration  radii  below  the interlayer spacing of MXene (~6 Å) show an order of magnitude slower permeation compared to single-charged cations [102]. At a space larger than the ion hydration radii, the binding affinity of sulfonate groups to the bivalent cations acts as the key separation mechanism [103]. When the pore radii are larger than the bare ion radii, but smaller than the ion hydration radii, partial dehydration largely contributes to the separation efficiency  of  the  membranes.  Polyethylene  terephthalate  (PET)  films with nanopores with fast transport of lithium ions have been attributed to the negatively charged nanopores with pore radii of ≈ 0.3 nm, and the selectivity  is  associated  with  the  dehydration  [103 -106].  The  exact separation mechanism is subject to critical analysis because of potential contribution of the defects in the active layer may not be reflected in the results [107].

texts.orig: Selective separation of Li + and other ions requires molecular scale control of nanomaterials of chemical/physical homogeneity. A full understanding of the dehydration of Li + ions, interaction of the pore geometry/chemistry with Li + ions is hindered by the lack of experimental analytical methods within the nanoscale spatial resolution [110]. Molecular simulations can gain some understanding of solute transport, but a full picture of the transport of ions through a realistic long pore is limited by the simulation capacity and membrane chemical heterogeneity. Materials stability is a top priority for any potential application of such  materials.  Particularly  for  the  salt-lake  brine,  the  highly  saline aqueous solution  would deteriorate  the  nanomaterials  [110].  Confirmation of robust chemical stability will dictate the fate of the research directions as the selected nanomaterials or morphological design would fail  during  frequent  chemical  cleaning  using  hypochlorite,  acid  and base. Molecular scale defect-free bulk membrane faces significant scientific and engineering challenges [101]. Additionally, a comprehensive view of the efficiency and cost of the process is required as the membrane performance is not the only evaluation criteria after being integrated within the process. In the following, an engineering aspect of the membrane development for nanofiltration membranes and monovalent selective IEMs will be discussed.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The steric hindrance, dielectric exclusion and Donnan exclusion are still  considered the mainstream separation mechanisms for designing the membranes for the separation of Mg 2 + and Li + from salt-lake brine [111,112]. Attempt for Mg/Li separation began with the commercial NF membranes, including Desal-5 DL, DK series (Supplementary Information Table S2). Various separation factors ranging from 3.5 to 20 have been obtained and the permeates have shown significantly lowered MLR [113]. Adjustment of the feed pH to below the isoelectric point (IEP) of a membrane surface attributes separation characters for Mg/Li using a negatively charged commercial NF membrane, thus Donnan exclusion plays the key role. The changes in pore size and dielectric characters are largely  neglected  which  may  affect  the  permselectivity  of  the  NF membrane. This leads to a new research direction for developing positively charged NF membranes as listed in Table S2. Other process parameters,  such  as  feed  ion  concentration,  temperature,  operational pressure, recovery rate, crossflow velocity, etc., are less critical for the control of the permselectivity, thus neglected in this account.

texts.orig: A  cross-linking  approach  has  been  reported  by  grafting  a  poly (dopamine) (PDA) layer onto a polyimide (PI) substrate followed by cross-linking polyethyleneimine (PEI) in ethanol with epichlorohydrin (ECH) as the cross-linker to form an active layer (Fig. 9A) [108]. The permeate MLR reached 5.0 from a feed of 65.3 at pH 6.5 using the West Taijnar  salt  lake.  Although  the  separation  performance  and  stability appear promising, the permeability (0.7 L m 2  h 1 bar 1 ) still requires further improvement. A more conventional approach is to impart positive charges to the monomer in an interfacial polymerization (IP) so that the resulting separation layer possesses permanent, positive charges to repel Mg 2 + . A piperazine (PIP) derivative, 1,4-Bis (3-aminopropyl)-piperazine (BAPP), has two aminopropyl groups bounded to the N atoms of the ring. The aqueous monomer BAPP rather than the organic monomer trimesoyl chloride (TMC) is found to be the key factor determining the reaction rate of IP because of its low reactivity [114]. The separation  factor  of  the  BAPP/TMC  composite  membrane  (2.6)  is slightly above that of the commercial NF90 membrane (2.1). Further Zwitterion-carbon nitride functionalized using BAPP also improves the permselectivity (Fig. 9B) and long-term stability has been observed by reducing MLR from 73 to 1.85 [115]. PEI has a large number of primary and secondary amine groups with an IEP of pH = 9.3, which means that at pH neutral, PEI is positively charged (Fig. 9C). PEI-based NF membranes can reduce the MLR from 20.0 to 1.3 at a separation factor of 20 [41].  The  adoption  of  a  CNC-COOH  interlayer  leads  to  a  Janus  NF membrane  with  both  hydrophobic/hydrophilic  properties  (Fig.  9E) [116]. The interlayer has a lower IEP (pH = 7.8) than traditional NF membranes  made  from  PEI  and  TMC  (pH = 8.6).  PIP  modified multi-walled  carbon  nanotubes  (M-CNTs)  can  also  improve  the  PEI membrane permeability by up to 14.0 L m 2  h 1 bar 1 , which is higher than that of most positively charged NF membranes [117]. By adding hydrophilic GO into the PES matrix, a ' bridge ' linking effect with the -NH2 in PEI has been discovered, which improves the interaction of PES and the active polyamide layer. Reduced skin layer thickness results in an increased flux and separation stability after 7 days [42].

texts.orig: The development of NF membranes with intrinsic positive charges is an  important direction  for  the  Mg/Li  separation  based  on  steric  and charge effect. These conventional designs may be dwarfed by the commercial NF membranes as adjustment of the pH of brine can alter the membrane surface charges from negative to positive. Commercial NF membranes have been utilized with success. The necessity of the new development  still  awaits  economical  evaluation.  The  introduction  of nanomaterials  into  this  type  of  NF  membrane  is  crucial  to  improve permeability  without  deteriorating  rejection.  However,  the  breakthrough is largely dependent on the progress of scientific understanding of separation mechanisms and technology advancement in preparation methodology.

texts.orig: Electrodialysis  has  been  utilized  to  produce  potable  water  from brackish water for more than 60 years [122]. Cost-efficient RO and NF have gradually replaced ED in the desalination market, but ED is still enjoying continuing growth as more challenging problems emerge in various industries. Lithium extraction from brine is one of the important cases. The IEMs, both cation exchange membranes (CEMs) with negative fixed  charges  and  anion  exchange  membranes  (AEMs)  with  positive fixed charges, are the key components in ED. texts.orig: For the salt-lake brine with a high LMR, a special ED configuration with monovalent-selective CEMs has been adopted [130,131]. Fig. 10E shows  the  structure  of  a  monovalent-selective  CEM,  consisting  of  a negatively charged CEM support and a positively charged function layer [130 -132]. This skin layer imposes an extra hindrance (energy barrier) for all cations in the transmembrane migration via Donnan exclusion. The higher electric potential barrier for Mg 2 + due to the higher charge density than that of Li + contributes to the improved selectivity between Li + and Mg 2 + [133]. The  CEM  is a cross-linked copolymer  of sulfonated styrene  and  divinylbenzene,  while  the AEM  is a copolymer of quaternary ammoniated  styrene and divinylbenzene. The corresponding structure illustrations  of (C) CR61  and (D) AR103. The hydrophobic backbone and hydrophilic charge groups demix on a microscale;  thus  interconnected  water-filled passages  (hydrophilic  zones)  are  supported  by  the  hydrophobic  structure, providing  desired  permeability  of  ions and  water  molecules  as  well  as  mechanical  and  chemical  robustness. (E) Scheme of a monovalent-selective CEM with the  positively  charged  skin  layer. Dimensions  are  not  to  scale. (F) Schematic diagram presenting the principle of a selective ED stack, which recovers lithium from feed solutions with Mg 2 + impurities.  The  elementary  repeating unit  (also  referred  to  as  a  cell  pair) consists  of  a  selective  CEM  (sCEM),  a diluent  spacer  (for  the  feed  brine),  an AEM, and a concentrate spacer (for the lithium-enriched  concentrate).  A  practical  ED  stack  usually  contains  tens  to hundreds of cell pairs between the only electrodes, thus the mass transfer inside the  stack  is  dominated  only  by  the repeating  cell  pairs. texts.orig: Some selected reports on the lithium ED extraction from the high MLR Chinese salt-lake brines using commercial CEMs are summarized in Table 4. The first commercial ED Li + extraction application was proposed  in  2003  using  commercial  monovalent-cation  selective  membranes  (Tokuyama  CMS)  [135].  Despite  the  great  impact  and  huge commercial  success,  the  technical  details  are  barely  disclosed,  likely because of the business interests. Therefore, another performance such as the current efficiency/energy consumption is not available in public literature. Recent bench tests show that the Selemion CSO membranes have high permselectivity of Li + over Mg 2 + ( P Mg Li = 18.8) and very low energy consumption (1.9 kWh kg 1 lithium) [136,137]. Using Neosepta CIMS from ASTOM Corporation, investigation on the effects of coexisting  cations  demonstrates  that  the  cations  migrate  across  the  CIMS membrane in the following permselectivity order: K + > Na + > Li + > Ca 2 + > Mg 2 + [139]. Results indicate that the CSO membranes exhibit overall better performance in permselectivity (18.8 vs . 8.6), current efficiency (37.3% vs . 8.68%), and energy consumption (1.9 vs . 30.3 kWh kg 1 lithium) than the CIMS membranes [39,40]. It is believed that this deviation is caused by the difference in the operation parameters, such as overall current density (Lower current density corresponds to higher current efficiencies) [137], the existence of monovalent  co-ions (monovalent co-ions reduce current efficiency) [139]. This conclusion is  very  important  as  a  fair  comparison  for  different  IEMs  for  Li + extraction is very important, particularly for the evaluation of the new membranes.

texts.orig: The commercial monovalent selective CEMs differentiate Li + from Mg 2 + . But the selectivity of monovalent ions is limited [139]. To achieve single  ion  selective  separation,  Li  ionic  superconductor-type  ceramic membranes (such as Li1 + x + yAlx(Ti/Ge)2 xSiyP3 yO12, (LixLay)TiOz and (LixLay)ZrOz)  have  been  investigated  for  the  extraction  of  Li + from seawater by membrane electrolysis [4,145 -147]. Polystyrene sulfonate threaded  MOF  membranes  showed  a  high  Li + conductivity  and  an excellent  Li + /Mg 2 + selectivity  of  1815  in  binary  solutions.  The  high selectivity stems from the sieving effect and the affinity difference of Li + and Mg 2 + ions to the sulfonate groups [103]. The resulting membrane ZIF-8  and  UiO-66  membrane  with  uniform  subnanometer  pores demonstrate a much higher LiCl/RbCl selectivity than traditional porous membranes  (~4.6 vs. ~0.6  for  ZIF-8,  and  ~1.8 vs. 0.8  for  UiO-66 membranes) [148]. Seamless, ultrathin ( < 500 nm) UiO-66-NH2  membranes on the surface of anodic aluminum oxide (AAO) and both high Li + /Mg 2 + selectivity  ( > 60)  and  excellent  membrane  stability  (five sequential ED cycles) are achieved [149].

texts.orig: More  engineering  approaches  by  blending  lithium  ion-selective materials [151 -154] and zeolite [155] with SPEEK have demonstrated Li + selectivities, such as lithium manganese oxide (LMO)/SPEEK, magnesium doped LMO/sulfonated polyethersulfone (SPES) [153] or SPEEK [152], or coating lithium selective adsorbent (LiCo0.5Mn1.5O4) particles on its  top  surface  by  in-situ  polymerization  method  [154].  Although promising permselectivity of Li + over other cations is reported, the experiments have not evaluated using Li + solution coexisted with cations as Mg 2 + , K + and Na + at high concentrations. It is suspected that real-life performance may be largely reduced. The long-term stability of these materials requires further engineered solutions.

texts.orig: The energy consumption is largely proportional to the applied current density, because the electrodialyzer is in reality an electric load of resistance. An extremely low current density corresponds to ultra-low energy  consumption,  but  not  cost-effective  because  the  large  membrane area equates to high capital investment. Conventional ED processes  operate  at  a  current  density  of  20 -100  mA  cm 2 [139,159], significantly higher than the values in these reports (Table 4). In the table salt production, monovalent cation (Na + ) is the dominating species in  seawater.  Such  a  high  concentration  of  Na + can  guarantee  a remarkably high operation current density for the membranes, which brings both high flux of Na + (good economics) and high monovalent selectivity.  While  in  lithium  extraction,  the  situation  is  much  more challenging. In the lithium-containing brines, the desired monovalent cation (Li + ) is only the minor species. The low concentration of Li + can only maintain a low operation current density. Simply increasing the current  density  will  remarkably  degrade  the  monovalent  selectivity. Although the lithium selectivity can be retained at relatively low current densities, the process cost will be much higher than that in salt production.  To  increase  the  current  density,  a  concentrated  brine  with elevated Li + concentration is needed. Furthermore, highly Li + ion selective membranes are desirable to break the current trade-off between the lithium flux and selectivity. Emerging Li + selective membranes (such as the SLM-ED hybrid, Li + ion superconductor membranes, and lithium ion-sieve  membranes)  are  promising  to  breakthrough  in  the  current technology bottlenecks. However, these technologies are still in their early stage and more in-depth research is needed to further evaluate the technical and economic feasibility.

texts.orig: Recent researches on supported liquid membranes (SLMs) reported the  application  of  an  electro-membrane  system  for  the  separation  of monovalent cations, i.e. Na + , K + and Li + [160]. Fig. 11A compares the bare sodium ions (0.95 Å) radius with that of potassium ion (1.33 Å), and their  similar  hydration  radius  (3.58  Å  and  3.31  Å,  respectively) (Table 2) [24]. Fig. 11B demonstrates an ion transfer at the organic solvent phase and water phase governed by the Gibbs free energy ( Δ G ) based on the electrostatic solvation of the ion. The Born equation gives the Δ G (kJ mol 1 ) of the transfer of an ion species of charge z and crystal radius r (in  Å)  from  phase  1  with  permittivity ϵ 1 to  phase  2  with permittivity ϵ 2.

texts.orig: Where NA Avogadro ' s number (6.02 × 10 23 ), e the elementary charge (1.6022 × 10 19 C) and ϵ 0 the permittivity of vacuum (8.854 × 10 12 F m 1 ). The pre-factors 695 and 20.3 in Eq. (10) result from transferring a monovalent cation ( z = 1) from the aqueous ( ϵ 1 = 80) into the nitrophenyl octyl ether (NPOE)/membrane phase ( ϵ 2 = 24) (Fig. 11B) [161]. For Li + ,  Na + and K + ,  the  calculated dehydration energy required for transferring into the NPOE/membrane phase are 33.8, 21.4, and 15.3 kJ mol 1 , respectively.  This  means  that  selectivity  between  ions  is achievable because of the difference in the dehydration energy of the relative ion species (refer to the equation imbedded in Fig. 11C). Further introduction of mobile charged molecules brings the ion exchange capacity into the membrane system as shown in Fig. 11D enabling the facilitated ion transport under  ED  with a high permselectivity. Compared  to  the  conventional  SLM  based  on  the  ion  concentration gradient, the proposed ED facilitated SLM system achieved an acceleration of ion transport. Design of such a membrane system is still under investigation, but holds strong technical promises because no complicated development of membrane materials is needed.

texts.orig: The  other  example  for  the  separation  of  ions  based  on  hybrid mechanisms is based on ion affinity to the active separation polymers. Polystyrene sulfonate (PSS) with sulfonate groups is a typical anionic polyelectrolyte for preparing layer-by-layer (LBL) membrane [162]. The affinity differences of sulfonate groups toward monovalent and divalent cations play very important roles in the membrane selectivity as the binding affinity of sulfonate groups to Mg 2 + ions ( = 3.18) is much greater than that of Li + ( = 1) [103]. Due to the condensation effect, counter-ions with higher binding affinities to the specific charge groups of the polymer  can  show  a  much  lower  permselectivity  [163].  Therefore,  it  is predicted  that  the  LBL  membrane  with  sulfonate  groups  will  have excellent lithium and magnesium separation performance.

texts.orig: The  above  examples  have  demonstrated  engineering  methods  for separating very similar cations by the combination of equilibrium (or quasi-equilibrium based on affinity) and non-equilibrium approaches. SLM [164,165], ED [122] and novel NF membranes prepared via LBL self-assembly  [166,167]  are  known,  which  are  able  to  scale-up  to membrane modules and even systems for membrane scientists and engineers. The expectation of these types of membrane design is rather promising as compared to a complicated precise control for the nanopore with nanomaterials [98,168 -170]. For Li + and Mg 2 + separation, membrane extraction (based on liquid-liquid extraction), nanofiltration and electrodialysis have their own distinct strengths and weaknesses.

texts.orig: FO is a spontaneous membrane process driven by osmotic pressure as in shown Fig. 12A. A draw solution (of high osmotic pressure) is separated from a feed solution of a lower osmotic pressure by an FO membrane, and the osmotic pressure difference drives water from the feed to the draw side across the membrane leading to the feed being concentrated  and  the  draw  solution  diluted.  If  the  circulation  energy  is neglected, FO requires nearly zero energy input [32]. Diluted draw solutions  can  be  discharged  to  the  salt-lake  for  further  evaporation  to regain the osmotic pressure. Reports on using commercial CTA and TFC FO membranes show a concentration factor  of  3  with  no  details  on rejection  [33].  Another  patent  discloses  an  adsorption-FO  process  to concentrate the LiCl brine from 200 mg/L to 12 wt%, at a rejection above 90% [172]. Commercial FO membranes have not been designed for such high salinity and frequently showed very low flux and relatively high reverse Mg 2 + flux [173]. Consequently, the greatest challenge for FO concentration of Li + brine is the membrane. An FO membrane with very high rejection to all ions (Li + , Na + , K + , Mg 2 + , etc.) is required to prevent the contamination of the lithium-enriched brine by the draw solutes and loss of Li + . Design low resistance and high rejection membranes for monovalent ions are of top priority. The second key criterion is the substrate of FO membranes as outlined below.

External concentration polarization (ECP) could be largely reduced to a comparably negligible level, thus it is not the key issue here. Reducing the impact  of  ICP  requires  optimization  of  the  substrate  morphology  to reduce the structure parameter. texts.orig: Another issue related to the high salinity is dehydration of the FO membrane (Fig. 12C). Dehydration occurs when the draw solution is in contact with the active layer while the substrate channel is still empty, water  in  the  membrane  pore  transports  out  driven  by  the  osmotic pressure,  leaving  the  substrate  dehydrated.  The  dehydrated  porous structure in practice imposes increased tortuosity, and increased S value followed by decreased water flux. If the substrate side is in contact with brine, dehydration will be prevented.

texts.orig: For the concentration purpose, an FO membrane requires an active separation layer allowing only water diffusion but blocking other ions. Inspired by biological proteins, aquaporin (AQP) has been attempted to improve the desalination membrane flux via embedding AQP vesicles in the TFC membrane [181,182]. By constructing artificial water channels (AWCs) into the TFC polyamide layer, a high water-to-salt permeability ratio has been achieved. These biomimetic membranes show a rejection of boron of 91.4% and for NaCl of 99.5% at 65 bar with a flux more than 75% higher than that of current best membranes [170]. Enlightened by this concept, we expect that the planar biomimetic selective layers could allow for dramatically improved salt rejection and permeance. Suspicion of defects created at the interface of the water-channels and substrate polyamide material is the origin of the low performance. A strategy for minimizing these defects by synthesis of an ultrahigh rejection membrane has been proposed via first formation of a smooth polyamide layer with  a  dense  layer  of  functional  groups,  which  then  assist  in  the bottom-up  polymerization  of  the  channel  insertion  matrix  [183].

texts.orig: 5. Confinement of lithium ion-sieve by membrane absorbers
texts.text: 5. Confinement of lithium ion-sieve by membrane absorbers

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Lithium ion-sieves cover a series of lithium selective adsorbents with unique  chemical  structures.  Fig.  2  shows  that  LIS  has  been  used  for extraction of Li + from high MLR salt-lake brine. There are typically three LIS types categorized by the chemical elements: (1) the lithium manganese oxides-type (LMO), MnO2 ⋅ xH2O (x = 0.3, 0.4, etc.) with a spinel structure [184]; (2) lithium titanium oxides-type (LTO), mainly Li2TiO3 with  a  layered  structure,  and  Li4Ti5O12 with  a  spinel  structure;  (3) lithium aluminum oxides-type (LAO), as LiX ⋅ 2Al(OH)3 ⋅ H2O (X = Cl , Br , NO3 , SO4 2 ) with layered structures [185]. LMO-type and LTO-type LISs have higher lithium ion adsorption capacity and selectivity, but concerns on the dissolution of metals and cost have largely hindered their applications. The adsorption capacity of LAO-type LISs is low, but their low cost, low toxicity and easy water desorption attribute them attractive for Li + absorption, and they are the only type at present with a capacity of 10 K LCE for production of Li2CO3 from the Chinese salt-lake brines. One critical problem for LISs is the breakage of the powder due to the change in the cavity in the adsorption and desorption cycles [185]. Loss of sieves and sorption capacity incur high cost and engineering problems. Granulation of the LISs has been a difficult technical problem [185]. Various methods have been researched to incorporate the LIS powder into polymer matrixes or inorganic materials to improve the adsorption process and reuse of LISs [29,151,153,186 -189].

The cost of the LISs has yet to be analyzed in comparison to the advantages of avoiding the loss of breaking LIS powder.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 6. Concluding remarks and outlook
texts.text: 6. Concluding remarks and outlook

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The rapid expansion of the EV industry worldwide has boosted a strong demand for lithium chemicals for LIBs. For the safe and healthy development of the Chinese EV industry, highly efficient technologies for the extraction of lithium ions from the Chinese salt-lake brine have been pursued with success, but still lagged  behind the  market  need. Because the Chinese salt-lake brine is characterized by very high MLRs, efficient and low-cost separation technologies for Mg 2 + and Li + ions have been researched for decades. Critical analysis of the current practice shows that the technologies encompass non-equilibrium approaches consisting  of  purification-concentration  steps,  using  core  membrane technologies such as NF, ED, and equilibrium approaches, LIS and LLE. The permselectivities of NF and ED membranes are determined by ion transport analogous to a reaction energy barrier, which is related to the steric,  electrostatic,  dielectric,  van  der  Waals,  frictional  and  viscous interaction. Nanomaterials have shown promise in preparing single ion selective  membranes,  but  concerns  on  the  defects  and  engineering technologies  for  large-scale  manufacturing  are  still  waiting  for  solid solutions. Present research on positive NF membranes and ion selective IEMS  are  limited  in  permselectivity  compared  to  commercial  membranes in some cases. For the concentration of the Li + enriched brine, FO has been proposed as one of the most energy and cost-efficient processes to reach a Li + content of 3 wt% in one step before production of lithium carbonate. Direction to the advanced FO membranes for highly saline brine is outlined as reduction in the structure parameter and the high permselective separation layer for water and ions.

texts.orig: Abbreviations liquid extraction is based on the Gibbs free energy of extraction. This review introduced a chemical exchange process to precisely control the purity of the Li + and the stripping acid concentration enables highly enriched Li + from feed of any concentration level. The negative environmental impact by loss of organic extractant in LLE is resolved by membrane extraction, which uses solvent and acid resistant polymeric membranes with interconnected nanoporous structures. The materials characteristics  and  membrane  preparation  processes  are  summarized including polymer blends and block copolymers and PEEK. Bench-scale results are promising using EVAL and PEEK membranes.
texts.text: Abbreviations liquid extraction is based on the Gibbs free energy of extraction. This review introduced a chemical exchange process to precisely control the purity of the Li + and the stripping acid concentration enables highly enriched Li + from feed of any concentration level. The negative environmental impact by loss of organic extractant in LLE is resolved by membrane extraction, which uses solvent and acid resistant polymeric membranes with interconnected nanoporous structures. The materials characteristics  and  membrane  preparation  processes  are  summarized including polymer blends and block copolymers and PEEK. Bench-scale results are promising using EVAL and PEEK membranes.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: For the short or medium term, the urgent need for lithium extraction technologies requires highly ion selective membranes and technologies with  application  potential.  Very  recent  research  on  combining  the nanomaterials with LBL technologies and supported liquid membrane with ED have shown exciting ion selectivity, without the need of any advanced instrumental fabrication technique. The lag in the science and technology development for designing single ion selective membranes from a non-equilibrium stance provides a great opportunity for equilibrium separation technologies, for instance, membrane extraction as well as the hybrid engineering solutions by combining merits of kinetic and  thermodynamic  controlled  separation  mechanisms.  The  review highlights  the  importance  of  membrane  technologies,  particularly highly selective membrane materials and processes, for the very challenging lithium extraction task from high MLR salt-lake brine. The review  also emphasizes  the  importance  of  imbedding  engineering considering and concept for a more tangible solution by avoiding pitfalls into  the  early  stage  of  research.  Beyond  the  application  in  lithium extraction from brine, the concept and ideas generated in this review will find many separating applications in much wider industries.
